key_insight: Liquid transport growth (LTG) uses a molten flux under a temperature gradient to decouple dissolution from crystallization, enabling high-yield single-crystal growth for materials with narrow growth windows or temperature-sensitive stoichiometry.
engineering_relevance: LTG offers a practical route to produce large, high-quality quantum-material crystals needed for property measurements and device-relevant studies, while reducing dependence on equilibrium solubility limits.
method: Use a horizontal furnace to create a controlled temperature gradient.
Keep dissolution and crystal precipitation spatially separated in the melt.
Tune growth time, melt stability, and ampoule geometry for the target compound.
evidence_signal: The abstract reviews representative LTG-grown quantum materials (including Fe3Sn2, CrTe3, UTe2, MoTe2, and WTe2) and uses them as qualitative evidence that the method broadens access to high-quality single crystals.
watch_item: LTG is still largely empirical, so growth outcomes can be sensitive to furnace design, gradient control, melt stability, and ampoule geometry.
paper_title: Recent progress on liquid transport growth of quantum materials
